coredata.dc:title: A fluorescent probe for the detection of Cu(II) in water and tumor cells
coredata.dc:creator: Qian, Di
Wang, Zheng
Xiao, Zhongxin
Fang, Chen-Jie
coredata.dc:description: A fluorescent Au nanocluster LRL−AuNCs modified with RGDK-containing peptide was synthesized with a simple one-pot method. Based on the “turn-off” model, LRL−AuNCs displayed high sensitivity and selectivity for sensing Cu2+. It showed a good linear calibration between the fluorescence intensity and Cu2+ concentration in the range of 5 ~ 80 μM (R 2 = 0.998), with the detection limit 3.9 μM. which is far lower than the maximum allowable concentration of Cu2+ (20 μM) in drinking water. Therefore, the detection of Cu2+ in water sample was performed in a handy way. As LRL−AuNCs showed low toxicity and good biocompatibility, LRL−AuNCs was used to sense the change of Cu2+ level in tumor cells by two-photon photoluminescence (TPPL) imaging. These results indicated that the LRL−AuNCs as a TPPL probe showed potential in tumor diagnosis and treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Au nanoclusters
Copper ions
Fluorescence quenching
Two-photon photoluminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence assay of the LRL−AuNCs to Cu2+
Sensing of Cu2+ based on a fluorescent LRL−AuNCs probe
Mechanism of fluorescence quenching by Cu2+
Detection of Cu2+ in water sample

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Natural Science Foundation of Beijing Municipality

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–Vis absorption spectra of the LRL−AuNCs in water. (B) IR spectra of LRL (black line) and LRL−AuNCs (red line). (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of LRL−AuNCs. (D) Stability of the LRL−AuNCs stored at 4 ℃. (E) XPS spectra of LRL−AuNCs. (F) XPS spectra in the Au 4f region of LRL−AuNCs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence response of the LRL−AuNCs to common metal ions (500 μM for K+, Na+ and Ca2+, and 50 μM for other ions). I and I0 were thefluorescence intensities monitored at λ em 665 nm with and without metal ions, respectively.
TEM images of the LRL−AuNCs before (A) and after addition of Cu2+ (B). The particle size distribution of the LRL−AuNCs before (C) and after addition of Cu2+ (D).

(A) Fluorescence spectrum (λ ex = 370 nm) of the LRL−AuNCs with addition of different concentrations of Cu2+ (0 ~ 80 μM). (B) Linear relationship between (I0-I)/I0 and the concentration of Cu2+. I and I0 were thefluorescence intensities monitored at λ em 665 nm with and without Cu2+, respectively. (C) The fluorescence image set of the LRL−AuNCs with addition of different concentrations of Cu2+. (D) Photograph of fluorescence test papers taken under irradiation by a 365 nm UV lamp.
The cell viability of A549 cells was incubated with different concentrations of the LRL−AuNCs for 24 h.
The TPPL images of Cu2+ in A549 cells. (A) The cells incubated with the LRL−AuNCs; (B) followed by incubation with 50 μM and (C) 100 μM Cu2+, respectively.
Determination of Cu2+ in tap water (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A fluorescent probe for the detection of Cu(II) in water and tumor cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutics, Capital Medical University, Beijing 100069, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmaceutics, Capital Medical University, Beijing 100069, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Core Facilities Center, Capital Medical University, Beijing 100069, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Core Facilities Center, Capital Medical University, Beijing 100069, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorescent Au nanocluster LRL−AuNCs modified with RGDK-containing peptide was synthesized with a simple one-pot method. Based on the “turn-off” model, LRL−AuNCs displayed high sensitivity and selectivity for sensing Cu2+. It showed a good linear calibration between the fluorescence intensity and Cu2+ concentration in the range of 5 ~ 80 μM (R 2 = 0.998), with the detection limit 3.9 μM. which is far lower than the maximum allowable concentration of Cu2+ (20 μM) in drinking water. Therefore, the detection of Cu2+ in water sample was performed in a handy way. As LRL−AuNCs showed low toxicity and good biocompatibility, LRL−AuNCs was used to sense the change of Cu2+ level in tumor cells by two-photon photoluminescence (TPPL) imaging. These results indicated that the LRL−AuNCs as a TPPL probe showed potential in tumor diagnosis and treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper ion (Cu2+) is an important component of organism and is also an essential catalytic cofactor for many kinds of enzymes [1-6]. However, excessive amount of Cu2+ shows high toxicity in cells [7] and could cause serious diseases including Wilson, cardiovascular disorders and Alzheimer’s disease (AD) [8,9]. Moreover, previous studies found that copper levels increase significantly in a variety of cancer patients (liver cancer, oral cancer, and prostate cancer, etc.) [10-12], and copper ions can promote tumor angiogenesis [13,14]. Therefore, it is particularly important to develop feasible method to detect copper ions with high sensitivity and specificity. Many methods, such as inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES), inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS), atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) have been developed to detect trace copper ions with high selectivity and sensitivity [15,16]. However, their practical application has been strongly limited by the need for expensive equipment, complex operating procedures, and longtime detection [17]. In contrast, fluorescence analytical methods have great potential because of its advantages of inexpensive measures, facile operation, and sensitive detection [18-21].

Recently, fluorescent gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) have attracted extensive attention, due to promising chemical and physical properties such as simple preparation, good biocompatibility, and high stability [22-26]. Specifically, the fluorescence characteristics of the AuNCs make them potential probe and sensor in metal ion and small molecule detection, as well in biological imaging [27]. The diameter of AuNCs is usually less than 2 nm, which is close to the Fermi wavelength of electrons, resulting in photoluminescence properties that can be adjusted from ultraviolet region to near-infrared region [28,29]. The photoluminescence property of AuNCs is closely related to the size of the gold core and the surface modification. Besides, it is easily affected by the surrounding environment [30]. For most AuNCs, the fluorescence emission is not only due to the inter-band (d-sp) transition, but also from the in-band (sp) transition across the HOMO-LUMO gap [31-33]. The surface ligands affect particularly the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs. It increases with more electron-rich groups such as amino and carboxyl into the ligand [34-37]. Besides, AuNCs have shown excellent nonlinear optical (NLO) properties, with its two-photon absorption cross section up to 105 GM [38]. Two-photon photoluminescence (TPPL) imaging has the advantages of little tissue damage, strong penetrating power, high resolution and high image contrast [39-44]. According to reports in the literature, most two-photon fluorescent probes are based on the FRET mechanism to achieve substance detection, with high sensitivity and specificity [45-47]. However, the synthesis of such fluorescent probes is usually more complicated. Since NLO feature of AuNCs is significantly superior to traditional organic dyes and quantum dots, the AuNCs as two-photon absorbers show potential in biological imaging.

In this work, we report water soluble gold nanoclusters LRL−AuNCs that synthesized by a simple one-pot method with LA−RGDK−LA (LRL) as both templet and ligand. LA−RGDK−LA is composed of lipoic acid (LA) and tetrapeptide RGDK (arginine, glycine, aspartic, and lysine). RGDK could specifically recognize the tumor cells [48-50], which endows LRL−AuNCs with tumor targeting ability. The sulfhydryl LA could connect to the surface of the AuNCs through Au−S bond. Thereby LRL−AuNCs were produced via fixing both ends of LA−RGDK−LA on the surface of AuNCs. The sensitivity and selectivity of the LRL−AuNCs in sensing Cu2+ was examined in the present work. The biocompatibility and toxicity of the LRL−AuNCs were assessed with MTT assays, and further its potential as a two-photon fluorescent probe in TPPL imaging for Cu2+ detection in tumor cells were also explored.

HAuCl4·3H2O, NaBH4, and 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5- diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were purchased from Sigma. LA−RGDK−LA was purchased from Jill biochemical Shanghai Co., Ltd. NaOH, HCl, HNO3, absolute ethanol, CuSO4·5H2O and other metal salts were obtained from Beijing chemical plant. [Cu(MeCN4)][PF6] was purchased from Macklin. Deionized water was used throughout the experiments.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were tested on a JEM-2100 microscope (JEOL, Japan). The fluorescence spectroscopy was analyzed on an F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi Ltd., Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded with UV2550 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by 250XI X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, UK). The TPPL images were recorded with the FVMPE-RS multiphoton laser scanning microscope (Olympus, Japan).

The LRL−AuNCs were fabricated by one-pot method based on the reported literature [51]. Typically, 1.0 mL of HAuCl4·3H2O (25 mM) was mixed with 3.0 mL of LA−RGDK−LA solution (4.25 mg/mL) under vigorous stirring at 25 ℃ for 2 min. and then added was 0.25 mL of NaOH solution (1.0 M). 0.12 mL of NaBH4 solution (300 mM) was added slowly to the above mixture and incubated for another 24 h under continuous stirring at 37 ℃. Finally, ethanol was added, and the mixture was centrifuged at 12, 000 rpm for 15 min and then stored at 4 ℃ for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of the LRL−AuNCs to Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: All the measurements of Cu2+ were tested in deionized water. 1000.0 µL different concentrations of Cu2+ solution was added to 20.0 µL of the diluted LRL−AuNCs solution, respectively. After incubated at 25 ℃ for 1 min, the fluorescence intensity of the mixture was measured by the fluorescence spectrometer.

To assess the selectivity of LRL−AuNCs to Cu2+, other common metal ions including K+, Na+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Cu+, Co2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+ and Ce3+ were selected.Different metal ions (500 μM for K+, Na+ and Ca2+, 50 μM for other metal ions, 1000.0 μL) and a diluted LRL−AuNCs solution (20.0 μL) were mixed separately and the mixture was incubated at 25 °C for 1 min. The fluorescence intensity of the mixture was recorded with the fluorescence spectrometer.

The practicability of the LRL−AuNCs was evaluated with tap water as a practical sample. 20 μL of LRL−AuNCs was added into 1.0 mL of tap water with exogenous Cu2+ ions (0, 10, 25, and 50 μM) for fluorescence detection.

To test the cytotoxicity of the LRL−AuNCs in vitro, A549 cells were selected. Firstly, the cells were cultured into a 96-well plate and incubated for 24 h until it adhered to the wall. After removal of the medium, the cells were incubated in cell medium for 24 h with different concentrations of the LRL−AuNCs (0, 10, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250 and 300 μM). Then, 20 μL of MTT (5 mg/mL) was added and incubated for 4 h. After that, 100 μL of DMSO was added to each well and the plate was shaken for 10 min to dissolve the formazan. The absorbance at 570 nm was measured with a microplate reader.

Before imaging, the cells were cultured into a 6-well plate and incubated for 24 h. Next, the LRL−AuNCs (250 μM) was introduced into 6-well plates and incubated for 3 h. Then, the Cu2+ solution (0, 50 and 100 μM) was added to the 6-well plate, and incubated for 30 min. The TPPL images were recorded with the multiphoton laser scanning microscope under excitation wavelength at 810 nm with a 575 ~ 645 nm detector.

The LRL−AuNCs were readily synthesized by mixing LA−RGDK−LA, HAuCl4 and NaBH4 in a one-pot process. After HAuCl4 was mixed with LA−RGDK−LA in basic solution, the light yellow color gradually faded away, indicating the formation of Au(I) complexes [52]. Next, the NaBH4 was used as a strong reductant to reduce gold ions into Au (0), and fluorescent LRL−AuNCs was fabricated. As shown in Fig. 1 A, no surface plasma resonance absorption was observed in 400 ~ 600 nm in the UV–Vis absorption spectra, which suggested that no large gold nanoparticles were formed. Fig. 1B shows the IR spectra of LA−RGDK−LA and LRL−AuNCs. The peaks at ~ 3294 cm−1 and 1633 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of NH and CO groups of LA−RGDK−LA, respectively. The stretching vibration and bending vibration of CH group appeared at 2391 cm−1 and 1435 cm−1. The absorption peak due to −(CH2)4 −of LA−RGDK−LA was observed at 721 cm−1. These characteristic peaks were observed in the infrared spectrum of LRL−AuNCs, indicating that RGD containing peptide ligand LA−RGDK−LA was modified on the surface of fluorescent gold nanoclusters. The LRL−AuNCs exhibited bright red fluorescence with an emission peak at 665 nm under 370 nm excitation (Fig. 1C). However, the fluorescence quantum yield of the LRL−AuNCs cannot be measured because the frequency-doubled peak of the excitation wavelength coincides with the emission peak. The LRL−AuNCs showed high stability without remarkable changes in fluorescence intensity after stored at 4 ℃ for 6 weeks (Fig. 1D).

The main elements of LRL−AuNCs were measured with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Specifically, the elements of Au, C, N, O, and S were detected from LRL−AuNCs (Fig. 1E).The peaks of Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2 were located at 84.3 and 88.0 eV, respectively (Fig. 1F). The binding energy of Au 4f peak could be assigned to Au(0) and Au(I) [53]. The ratio of Au(I) to Au(0) was calculated to be 3.9:1. As high Au(I) ratio is beneficial to enhancing the interactions between Au(I) and Au(I) and thus improving quantum yield [32,54,55], the fluorescent LRL−AuNCs were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of Cu2+ based on a fluorescent LRL−AuNCs probe
ce:section.ce:para:
Cells live in a complex environment, which brings a great challenge for specifically monitoring intracellular analytes [56]. To explore the selectivity of LRL−AuNCs probe to Cu2+, the fluorescence response of LRL−AuNCs to thirteen common metal ions (K+, Na+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Cu+, Cu2+, Co2+, Al3+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+ and Ce3+)was examined (Fig. 2
). Only Cu2+ effectively quenched the fluorescence of LRL−AuNCs, while other common metal ions did not show obvious quench effect. The result indicated good selectivity of LRL−AuNCs probe toward Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of fluorescence quenching by Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the mechanism of fluorescence quenching by Cu2+, we first observed micromorphological changes of LRL−AuNCs in sensing through transmission electron microscope (TEM) images. It is observed from the TEM images

that the pristine LRL−AuNCs are spherical ( Fig. 3 A) with narrow size distribution and monodispersity and it aggregated after addition of Cu2+ (Fig. 3B), with an average diameter of 2.2 ± 0.3 nm (Fig. 3C) enlarged to 35.0 ± 6.8 nm (Fig. 3D), as judged from image analysis of 200 individual particles respectively. It indicates that LRL−AuNCs aggregated through the metal chelation interaction after addition of Cu2+, which further resulted in fluorescence quenching [57,58].

To verify the sensing mechanism, the chelating agent Na2EDTA (Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt) was used to study the fluorescence recovery of LRL−AuNCs. The fluorescence intensity of the LRL−AuNCs in the presence of Cu2+ could be gradually recovered after adding excessive EDTA, because of the stronger chelating coordination between Cu2+ and EDTA ( Fig. S1 ). It was confirmed that fluorescence quenching is related to the bound Cu2+ but not to free metal ions [59], which caused some energy transfer. Specifically, the paramagnetic ions could facilitate intersystem crossing (ISC) [17,60,61]. For the LRL−AuNCs which containing free –NH2 and COOH groups on the gold core, the paramagnetic Cu2+ bound to the peptides via coordinated with –NH2 and COOH, the excited electron of the gold core lost its energy via ISC, resulting in the quenching of the fluorescence.

The response of the LRL−AuNCs to different concentrations of Cu2+ was evaluated by measuring the fluorescence intensity. When the amounts of Cu2+ were increasing, the fluorescence intensity of LRL−AuNCs at 665 nm was gradually quenched (Fig. 4 A). Fig. 4B illustrates the linear regression curve of LRL−AuNCs, which was established by plotting the relationship between the reduced ratio (I0-I)/I0 of fluorescence intensity and the added concentrations of Cu2+. The results suggested that LRL−AuNCs could detect Cu2+ in a range of 5 to 80 µM (R 2 = 0.998) and the detection limit was 3.9 μM (3σ, σ is the standard deviation). This value of limit of detection (LOD) is far lower than the maximum allowable concentration of Cu2+ (20 μM, equivalent to 1.25 ppm) in drinking water (established by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency [62]).

To explore the feasibility in the practice for rapid and direct observation, we further performed the direct visual detection under a 365 nm UV lamp. Fig. 4C indicates that the red fluorescence faded away gradually with increasing the amounts of Cu2+. When the concentration of Cu2+ reached 50 µM, we could distinguish the color change with naked eye. We further prepared gold nanoclusters detection test papers with filter paper strips. As shown in Fig. 4D, the red fluorescence of LRL−AuNCs gradually weakened in the presence of Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ in water sample
ce:section.ce:para: According to the World Health Organization (WHO) drinking water quality guidelines, copper is identified as an important chemical element in drinking water [63]. The analysis of Cu2+ in tap water was established using the LRL−AuNCs to explore the applicability in practice. The analysis results were listed in Table 1 . The method showed a good repeatability and accuracy, with the mean recovery of Cu2+ ranged from 94.0% to 104.4% and the relative standard deviations ranged from 1.2% to 3.9%. Thus, this detection method provided a good application prospect in detecting the concentration of Cu2+ in environmental water.

cells. After adding 50 μM Cu2+ to A549 cells, the red fluorescence became weaker (Fig. 6B), which reveals that the nanoprobe could sense Cu2+ in cells. When the concentration of Cu2+ reached 100 μM, the red fluorescence almost completely disappeared (Fig. 6C). These experiments demonstrated the potential of LRL−AuNCs as TPPL imaging probe in detecting Cu2+ in vitro.

In summary, an emissive gold nanoclusters LRL−AuNCs was readily fabricated by a simple one-pot method, and it could be used for the detection of copper ions and TPPL probe imaging in vitro. The fluorescent probe for Cu2+ showed high selectivity and sensitivity, due to the aggregation-induced turn-off fluorescence. Based on the above characteristics, the LRL−AuNCs probe was used to detect copper ions in water samples. Furthermore, it has advantages of low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, and thus it could be applied in imaging and sensing of Cu2+ in tumor cells by TPPL imaging.

Di Qian: Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Zheng Wang: Investigation, Methodology. Zhongxin Xiao: Investigation, Methodology. Chen-Jie Fang: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the NSFC (21571133, 21171120), Beijing Natural Science Foundation Program and Scientific Research Key Program of Beijing Municipal Commission of Education (KZ201710025024), Natural Science Foundation of Beijing Municipality (7132020).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2021.108471.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
